author: Maya (mum)
title: Tooth-brushing meltdown again
text: Third night this week. She screamed the electric brush was 'drilling her teeth'. Ended up with the soft manual one and a song. 20 min to do a 2-min job. I'm wrecked.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Calm morning — noted it
text: Ate breakfast, got dressed first try, walked to school holding my hand. Told her I was proud. She shrugged but smiled. Writing it down so I remember it's possible.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Appetite gone on the stim
text: Day 9 of methylphenidate. She picked at dinner, left most of it. Growth chart is in the back of my mind. Will flag with Dr Kostas at the review.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Sensory win — new headphones
text: Big W had the kid-size noise-cancelling ones on sale. She wore them in the shopping centre and actually walked next to the trolley. No meltdown. These are staying in the bag.

author: Maya (mum)
title: School gate drama
text: Something happened with Amelia at pickup. She wouldn't talk about it in the car. Cried for 10 min once we got home, then asked for a hug. Didn't push her.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Screen time transition
text: iPad off at 4pm. Usual explosion. 12 minutes of escalation. New plan from Jess: 5-min countdown with a visual timer. Will try tomorrow.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Timer worked
text: Visual timer for iPad. Two small protests, then handed it over. Told Jess this worked.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Bedtime — good one
text: Weighted blanket + story + red light. Asleep by 8:40. That is early for her.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Rainy day = bouncing off walls
text: Stuck inside. Ran laps in the hallway. Trampoline would have fixed this in 10 minutes. Need an indoor plan B.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Refused dinner — broccoli
text: Cried at the texture. Ate rice plain. I don't know if this is sensory or stim appetite loss. Feels like both.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Kind to Jamie
text: Shared her dinosaur puzzle with her little brother without being asked. He's 3. She's been rough with him lately. Wanted to write it down.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Footy grand final — overstimulated
text: Took her to a family lunch with the game on loud TV, 12 people, all adults chatting. Lasted 40 min before a full meltdown. Left early. Lesson: headphones next time, and an exit plan.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Homework — reading
text: 10 minutes of reading the sight word list. She wanted to stop after 3. I let her. Jess said picking battles.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Q for Jess next week
text: She's been chewing her shirt collars again. Two tops ruined. Is there a chewy I can get her for school? Ask Jess.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Morning routine chart
text: Put up the visual chart Jess made. She ticked boxes herself. Got out the door 8 min earlier. Skeptical it'll last but noting it.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Saturday was hard
text: Birthday party at a trampoline park. She wanted to go, then shut down at the door from the noise. Sat in the car for 20 min. Went in with headphones, lasted 30 min, left. She cried the whole way home because she 'wanted to be normal'. Gutted.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Med dose question
text: She said the stim makes her feel 'tight in her chest'. Probably nothing but I want to ask Dr Kostas.

author: Maya (mum)
title: School said she had a good day
text: Ms Harper emailed. 'Great focus in literacy today.' Screenshotted it, put it on the fridge.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Sleep onset — 90 min
text: Took her 90 minutes to fall asleep. Third time this week. Wonder if it's the afternoon dose timing.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Swimming lesson — yes
text: She LOVED it. Water + pressure + structure. This is our thing. Booking every Saturday.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Forgot headphones
text: Coles at 5pm with no headphones was a catastrophic planning error. I'll know for next time.

author: Maya (mum)
title: OT chewy arrived
text: Necklace-style chewy from Amazon. She's been wearing it to school. Collars are safe this week.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Emotion word — used 'frustrated'
text: Amit has been working on emotion labels. She said 'I'm frustrated with Jamie' instead of hitting him. Real thing. Writing it down.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Christmas concert — left early
text: She sang 2 songs then covered her ears and walked off stage. Ms Harper handled it beautifully. I was proud she lasted as long as she did.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Med holiday plan
text: Dr Kostas said a short med break over Christmas is OK. We'll try 4 days off, see what happens.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Day 2 off meds
text: Bouncing, loud, impulsive — all back. But eating like a horse. Two sides to this coin.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Day 4 off meds — restart Monday
text: She said 'my brain is too fast today'. Self-aware. Restarting Monday as planned.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Christmas morning — survived
text: Too many presents, too fast. 40 min meltdown at 10am. Retreated to her room with the weighted blanket. Came out at lunch like nothing happened.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Beach trip — unexpected win
text: Torquay for the day. Hours in the sand. Digging holes, no meltdowns. Heavy work + outdoors + space = gold.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Holiday is wearing thin
text: Too unstructured. She's snappy, I'm snappy. Back to school can't come fast enough. Fought with Jamie over a Lego piece for 45 minutes.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Year 2 anxiety
text: Started asking if the new teacher would be 'mean'. Couldn't sleep. We made a visit plan to see the classroom before term starts.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Classroom visit — helped
text: Went in with Mr Doyle. She chose where she wants to sit (near the window, away from the door). Much calmer after.

author: Maya (mum)
title: First day of Year 2
text: Walked in with her chewy, headphones in the bag, morning dose on time. Held it together. Came home shattered and silent. Bath + quiet = recovery.

author: Maya (mum)
title: She asked about sleepovers
text: Wants to stay at Amelia's. First time she's asked. I'm nervous about the unfamiliar environment. Will ask Amit.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Friday meltdown after school
text: She held it together all week and blew up at the front door at 3:15. Screaming, hitting the wall. 25 minutes. This is the pattern. She's masking all week.

author: Maya (mum)
title: School said it's going well
text: Mr Doyle emailed. She's using the sensory corner twice a day. Proud of her for asking.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Valentine's cards
text: Made cards for three kids. Specific about who got the pink one. Sweet.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Stomach ache — acute
text: Woke up at 2am crying with stomach pain. Lasted about an hour. GP in the morning. She's OK now but I'm watching.

author: Maya (mum)
title: GP visit
text: Dr Tran said likely constipation from the stim. Suggested more water and fibre. Flagged to Dr Kostas.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Friendship stuff — Amelia and Zara
text: Three-way dynamic. She says Zara 'took Amelia away'. Big feelings. Not sure how much to intervene.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Did her homework without a fight
text: First time all year. 20 minutes of reading. I didn't make a big deal of it. Writing it down.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Sleep onset trouble again
text: Back to 60-90 min to fall asleep. She's been asking to read later. Wondering if we're overdoing the afternoon dose.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Excursion to the zoo
text: Full school day, long bus, big crowds. Came home in pieces. Screamed at me for parking in the 'wrong spot'. I didn't. Recovery mode all evening.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Birthday coming up
text: She wants a small party. 4 kids, backyard, no music. Smart kid — she knows what she can handle.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Birthday party — went well
text: 4 kids, cake, bubbles in the backyard. No meltdowns. She asked for quiet time in her room twice and then came back out. Self-regulation.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Stomach again
text: Mild this time. Water + pear + walk. Resolved by dinner. Still tracking.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Spelling test — upset about mistakes
text: Got 8/10 and cried for 15 min about the two she missed. Perfectionism is real. Amit flagged this last session.

author: Maya (mum)
title: She told me she's 'wired different'
text: At bedtime. 'My brain is wired different to Jamie's.' We talked about it. She's proud of the 'fast' part, sad about the 'too much' part. She's 6 and saying this.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Holidays week 1 — OK
text: Kept routine tight. Swim Monday, park Tuesday, OT Wednesday, playdate Thursday. Structure is the whole game.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Play date sensory overload
text: Went to a kid's house with 3 dogs, a toddler and a loud TV. Lasted 40 min. She said 'too much' and asked to leave. I was impressed she asked.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Back to school in 5 days
text: Anxiety creeping back. She asked if the teacher would remember her. We looked at photos of Year 2 so far. Calmed her down.

author: Maya (mum)
title: Meltdown at the dentist
text: Bright lights, the chair reclining, the water suction noise. Couldn't do the clean. Dentist was patient. Will book an early-morning slot next time.

author: Dr A. Kostas (Paediatrician)
title: Commencement of methylphenidate — initial review
text:
PAEDIATRIC REVIEW — Ellie (DOB [redacted]), 6y 4m.

Attending: Mother (Maya). Second visit following commencement of immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg mane on 2025-10-11. Two-week review.

Presenting concerns (as discussed with mother):
Ellie was commenced on stimulant medication following the initial diagnostic formulation of ADHD combined-type and confirmed sensory processing differences. This review is scheduled to assess early response, tolerability, and any emerging adverse effects prior to decisions about titration.

Maternal report: Mother reports a subjectively 'noticeable' reduction in impulsive behaviours at home, particularly around meals and shared activities with her younger brother (Jamie, 3y). She describes improved task completion on routine items — getting dressed, packing a school bag — where previously Ellie would abandon the task within 30–60 seconds. Homework reading (decodable readers, approximately 10 minutes) is now achievable in a single sitting on most evenings, whereas pre-medication it required two to three interrupted attempts and typically ended with parental frustration.

Adverse effects: Appetite suppression has been observed and is clinically significant. Mother reports Ellie refusing lunch at school on at least 3 of the last 10 school days, picking at dinner, and requesting a larger breakfast (currently the meal at which she is most receptive). There is no parent-reported nausea, headache, or abdominal discomfort. Sleep onset has not lengthened. No tics observed or reported. No cardiovascular symptoms (palpitations, chest discomfort, unexplained shortness of breath). Mood described as 'more settled' in the mornings with some 'quieter' periods in the late afternoon that may represent medication wear-off or rebound.

Examination: Alert, interactive, eye contact maintained. BP 98/64 (25th–50th centile for age). HR 92 regular. Weight 19.8 kg (50th centile, stable from baseline of 19.9 kg). Height 116.4 cm (50th–75th centile). No cardiac murmurs. Chest clear. Abdomen soft, non-tender. Neurological examination within normal limits.

Impression: Partial response to 5 mg mane methylphenidate. Appetite suppression is present but not yet affecting weight trajectory. Tolerability otherwise good.

Plan:
1. Continue immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg mane.
2. Add 2.5 mg midday dose contingent on school-reported afternoon attention drop. Review after fortnight of split dosing.
3. Dietary strategy: high-calorie breakfast, preferred foods at dinner, permit 'grazing' after evening dose wear-off.
4. Growth monitoring: weight and height at each review, plot on centile charts, flag any drop of more than one major centile.
5. Mother to maintain a medication/behaviour log — dose, timing, observations, meals — to bring to next review.
6. Review in 6 weeks (2025-12-06) unless earlier concern.
7. OT referral in progress (Dr Tran). Psychiatry review with Dr Meyer scheduled 2025-11-02.

Safety-netting: Mother counselled to contact the practice or attend emergency department for any new cardiovascular symptoms, persistent headache, sudden mood or behavioural change, or weight loss exceeding 1 kg within a fortnight.

Copy to: Dr L. Tran (GP), Dr R. Meyer (Child Psychiatrist), family file.

author: Dr R. Meyer (Child Psychiatrist)
title: Initial psychiatric assessment
text:
INITIAL PSYCHIATRIC ASSESSMENT — Ellie, 6y 4m.

Referrer: Dr A. Kostas (Paediatrician). Attending: Mother (Maya). Father absent from session (work commitments) — telephone consultation offered, mother declined at this stage, will consider for follow-up.

Reason for referral: Formal diagnostic formulation and pharmacological review following paediatrician-initiated stimulant trial. Clarification of any co-occurring anxiety, mood, or developmental psychopathology.

History of presenting complaint: Ellie is a 6-year-old girl in Year 1 at Brunswick Primary, living with her mother, father, and 3-year-old brother Jamie. Mother describes a 'hurricane brain' that has been present since toddlerhood but became functionally impairing once structured schooling commenced. Prominent features include: marked inattention to non-preferred tasks, motor restlessness (inability to remain seated through a family meal, constant fidgeting, climbing on furniture), impulsive verbal interruption in conversation and classroom, rigidity around transitions (particularly screen-to-non-screen), and strong sensory-driven avoidance behaviours around grooming (tooth-brushing, hair-washing), loud environments, and unfamiliar textures. School observations corroborate (see prior report, Ms J. Reilly, Year 1).

Developmental history: Full-term uncomplicated delivery. Milestones achieved within expected windows — walking 12 months, first words 14 months, sentences by 24 months. Early language atypically advanced. Sleep has been a theme since infancy, with a persistent 45–60 minute sleep-onset latency pre-medication. Toilet trained by 3y. No history of regression, no clinical features of autism spectrum disorder on structured history or observation — reciprocal social engagement is developmentally appropriate, imaginative play present, no circumscribed interests, no stereotypies.

Family history: Maternal cousin with diagnosed ADHD on stimulant. Paternal grandmother with generalised anxiety disorder. No family history of bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, or eating disorder.

Current medication: Immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg mane (commenced 2025-10-11). Early response characterised by partial improvement in impulsivity and task persistence; appetite suppression present (see paed review).

Mental state examination: Ellie engaged readily. Appearance appropriate, well-kempt, dressed in bright colours. Psychomotor — restless but not distressed, frequent fidgeting with clinic toys, left her chair four times during the 50-minute session. Speech — rate slightly pressured, articulation appropriate for age, vocabulary above average. Mood — self-reported 'good', affect euthymic with appropriate reactivity. Thought form — goal-directed with occasional tangential drift. Thought content — no delusional material, no obsessional content, no suicidal or self-harm ideation. Perception — no hallucinations elicited. Cognition — attention limited in the clinic setting, consistent with presentation. Insight — developmentally appropriate, describes her brain as 'fast'. Judgement — age-appropriate.

Standardised measures: Conners-3 Parent and Teacher forms returned (elevated T-scores >75 on Inattention and Hyperactivity/Impulsivity subscales, both raters). SCAS Parent Report — scores within normal limits, no subscale above clinical threshold. Sensory Profile 2 — previously completed by OT; findings consistent with 'much more than others' for Sensory Sensitivity and Sensory Avoiding.

Impression (DSM-5-TR formulation):
- ADHD, Combined Presentation, Moderate severity (314.01 / F90.2). Full criteria met across two settings (home, school), with onset before age 12, duration >6 months, significant functional impairment.
- Co-occurring sensory processing differences (not a distinct DSM disorder; documented for treatment planning).
- No current anxiety disorder meeting DSM-5 threshold. Emergent perfectionism noted on history (distress following minor errors) — below clinical threshold but worth monitoring; anxiety-prevention work indicated.
- No mood or psychotic disorder. No suicidality.
- No features of autism spectrum disorder on structured history or observation.

Treatment plan:
1. Continue stimulant regimen under paediatrician titration (Dr Kostas leading).
2. No additional pharmacotherapy at this stage. Would not recommend anxiolytic or antidepressant in the absence of a threshold condition.
3. Psychology referral for early intervention around emotion regulation, perfectionism scripts, and parent-directed behavioural scaffolding. Will provide list of paediatric-specialist clinicians.
4. Continue OT input (Jess P.).
5. Psycho-education provided to mother — ADHD heritability, expected trajectory, realistic expectations re: pharmacological effect size.
6. Review at 6 months (2026-04-01) or sooner at referrer's or family's request.

Copy to: Dr A. Kostas, Dr L. Tran, family file.

author: Dr L. Tran (GP)
title: Referral — paediatric occupational therapy
text:
REFERRAL — Paediatric Occupational Therapy.

To: Jess P., Senior Paediatric Occupational Therapist, Northcote Therapy Collective.
Re: Ellie (DOB [redacted]), 6 years 4 months, Year 1, Brunswick Primary.
Medicare item: 10956 (allied health under CDM plan, 5 sessions). Care plan number and billing details attached.

Dear Jess,

Thank you for accepting Ellie for occupational therapy assessment and intervention. I have been her general practitioner since birth and am writing with the consent of her mother, Maya, who will attend sessions.

Clinical summary: Ellie was diagnosed in October 2025 with ADHD, combined-type, by Dr A. Kostas (paediatrician) and had that diagnosis confirmed on formal psychiatric assessment by Dr R. Meyer on 2 November 2025 (letter copied here for your reference). She was commenced on immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg mane on 11 October 2025 and is tolerating treatment with partial response and expected appetite suppression.

Sensory-processing concerns are the primary reason for this referral. Mother reports longstanding difficulties across several sensory channels:

- Auditory: Ellie is highly reactive to unexpected loud sounds (school bells, hand driers, traffic noise). Mother reports she has recently begun using child-sized noise-cancelling headphones in shopping centres with dramatic improvement in tolerability — willing to walk beside the trolley rather than bolting or melting down.
- Tactile: Significant avoidance around tooth-brushing (particularly electric toothbrushes, which Ellie describes as 'drilling my teeth'), hair-washing, clothing tags and seams, and certain food textures. These avoidances are not new and pre-date the medication trial.
- Vestibular/proprioceptive: Strong seeking behaviours — jumping, climbing, deep pressure. Mother reports Ellie is calmest after 10–15 minutes of 'heavy work' (carrying groceries, pushing a wheelbarrow).
- Oral: Some texture-selective eating, though not at the severity of ARFID.

Functional impact: The current pain points (mother's words) are grooming routines, transitions (especially from screens), and a late-afternoon 'meltdown' pattern at school pickup which may represent end-of-day sensory overload rather than medication rebound.

School context: Ms Reilly (Year 1 teacher) has provided a written report describing classroom over-arousal in music and PE, improved focus in morning small-group work, and some difficulty maintaining attention after lunch. The school is receptive to reasonable adjustments once recommendations are formalised.

Goals for OT (family-agreed):
1. Sensory profile (I suggest Sensory Profile 2 caregiver form, plus clinic observation) with written report we can share with the school.
2. Classroom regulation strategies — sensory break protocol, use of permitted tools (chewy, fidget, headphones) within school policy.
3. Home routine support — morning checklist, grooming desensitisation programme, transition scripts.
4. Handwriting/grip assessment if appropriate (mother notes fatigue on writing tasks).
5. Parent coaching — heavy-work integration, proprioceptive input at home.

Concurrent care: Paediatrician Dr A. Kostas (titration and growth monitoring). Child psychiatrist Dr R. Meyer (6-monthly review, no current pharmacotherapy beyond stimulant). Psychology referral to be initiated separately once a clinician with paediatric ADHD experience is identified.

Please copy session notes and any formal reports to me and to Dr Kostas. I have discussed consent for inter-professional communication with mother and she is in agreement.

Happy to discuss by phone if useful — the practice number is below.

Kind regards,
Dr L. Tran, MBBS, FRACGP
Copy to: Dr A. Kostas, Dr R. Meyer, family.

author: Dr A. Kostas (Paediatrician)
title: Medication titration note
text:
PAEDIATRIC REVIEW — Ellie, 6y 5m. Six-week review, methylphenidate titration.

Attending: Mother. Medication log reviewed (20 entries since last visit).

Interval history: Since the last review (2025-10-25), Ellie has continued on immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg mane. Mother reports consistent morning response — improved on-task time for homework, more compliant with morning routine (though morning checklist adherence dropped off around week 3, a pattern the OT (Jess P.) has flagged separately as common and is currently iterating).

The principal new concern is an afternoon attention drop. Ms Reilly (Year 1 teacher) has emailed twice to note inconsistent afternoon engagement, particularly after 14:00. Mother reports an analogous 2–3pm pattern at home on weekends and non-school days — task persistence drops, motor restlessness returns, and several of the presentation features (interrupting, climbing) re-emerge.

This pattern is consistent with the pharmacokinetic profile of immediate-release methylphenidate (duration of effect approximately 3–4 hours from administration) and represents wear-off rather than tolerance.

Tolerability:
- Appetite: Remains suppressed at lunch. Dinner intake improved in the past fortnight after mother shifted dinnertime to 18:30 (after medication wear-off). Breakfast intake good.
- Sleep: Onset currently 30–45 minutes (within normal range for age). No insomnia reported.
- Mood: Stable. Mother has noted occasional 'quiet' spells at 15:30–16:30 which she interprets as wear-off rebound rather than low mood. No tearfulness, no self-deprecatory talk.
- Cardiovascular: Nil. No palpitations, no chest pain, no headache.
- Tics: None observed or reported.
- Behavioural rebound: Mild. Mother manages with a structured afternoon snack and outdoor time.

Examination: Alert, cooperative. BP 100/66. HR 88 regular. Weight 19.6 kg — a decrease of 0.2 kg over six weeks. Height 116.6 cm. Both parameters remain within 50th centile, no centile-line crossing. Growth chart updated and discussed with mother. Cardiac exam unremarkable. No tic movements observed over 30-minute consultation.

Impression: Partial response to 5 mg mane with pharmacokinetic wear-off driving afternoon symptom re-emergence. Appetite suppression present without clinically significant growth impact at this time.

Plan:
1. Add immediate-release methylphenidate 2.5 mg at 12:00 (post-lunch, school-administered via locked medication register). Continue 5 mg mane. Total daily dose 7.5 mg.
2. Dietary intervention: Introduce a structured pre-medication high-calorie breakfast (options discussed — full-fat dairy, nut butters if safe, avocado). Permit post-wear-off 'grazing' window 17:00–19:30.
3. Growth: Re-weigh at next review. Flag for dietitian referral if weight loss exceeds 500 g over the next 6 weeks, or if a major centile line is crossed.
4. Sleep: Monitor onset latency on split-dose regimen. Re-evaluate if onset exceeds 60 minutes consistently — would consider moving midday dose earlier.
5. Coordinate with school to confirm administration logistics. Letter to school nurse and class teacher to follow under separate cover.
6. Review 4 weeks (2026-01-06).
7. No change to concurrent care — OT ongoing, psychiatrist review scheduled April 2026, psychology referral in progress.

Safety-netting reiterated. Copy to: Dr L. Tran, Dr R. Meyer, Jess P. (OT), family file.

author: Dr A. Kostas (Paediatrician)
title: Medication holiday — consent discussed
text:
PAEDIATRIC CONSULT — Ellie, 6y 6m. Medication holiday consultation.

Attending: Mother. Teleconsult (15 minutes).

Reason for consult: Family has requested a planned brief medication holiday over the Christmas school break. The request is principally motivated by (a) a desire to support appetite recovery ahead of school return, (b) clinical curiosity about the current off-medication behavioural baseline after 10 weeks of treatment, and (c) a family wedding on 20 December at which mother reports she would like to observe Ellie's unmedicated interaction with extended family members.

Clinical background: Ellie has been on immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg mane (since 2025-10-11) with the addition of 2.5 mg midday (since 2025-12-06). Partial clinical response, afternoon wear-off managed by the split dose, appetite suppression ongoing but weight stable.

Risks discussed with mother (documented in consultation):
1. Rebound symptoms — attention, impulsivity, and motor restlessness will re-emerge rapidly (within 24 hours) and may be more prominent in the early off-medication days.
2. Behavioural escalation in response to the social demands of a holiday period — Christmas gatherings, unfamiliar sensory environments, disrupted routine.
3. Social disruption — Ellie may find large family events dysregulating and this could colour how she is perceived by extended family who have not seen her since the diagnosis.
4. Sleep — onset latency may change (likely lengthen).
5. Risk of the family extending the 'holiday' informally and inadvertently discontinuing treatment. Mother assured me there is no such intention.

Benefits discussed:
1. Appetite recovery window and catch-up on any mild caloric deficit.
2. Clinician and family opportunity to observe off-medication baseline after 10 weeks of consolidated treatment. This is clinically useful as it supports shared decision-making about long-term treatment.
3. Break from the daily medication routine during a period when school-based dosing logistics are irrelevant.

Shared decision: Agreed on a structured 4-day medication holiday across 17–20 December inclusive. Resume 7.5 mg split dose on 22 December to allow two days of medicated behaviour prior to Christmas Day.

Operational plan:
1. Mother to maintain a structured daily log across the 4 off-medication days — sleep, appetite (three meals + snacks), behaviour (one-line descriptor per half-day), any meltdowns or wins, any concerning adverse events.
2. Mother to contact my rooms or attend ED if any acute behavioural safety concern (self-harm ideation, aggression toward sibling, or injury risk) arises during the off-medication period.
3. Resume split dose (5 mg mane + 2.5 mg at 12:00) on 22 December. Breakfast intake should be prioritised on the resumption day.
4. Next scheduled review pre-Year 2 (2026-01-20), at which point we will discuss whether any further adjustments are warranted ahead of the more demanding Year 2 curriculum. Mother to bring the medication holiday log to that review.

Consent documented: Mother consents to the plan as described. She has been provided with my after-hours contact details and the after-hours nurse line.

Copy to: Dr L. Tran, family file.

author: Dr A. Kostas (Paediatrician)
title: Pre-Year 2 review
text:
PAEDIATRIC REVIEW — Ellie, 6y 7m. Pre-Year 2 review.

Attending: Mother.

Interval history (past 6 weeks): Ellie completed a structured 4-day medication holiday 17–20 December as agreed (DL-005). Mother's log (reviewed) documents expected rebound on days 1–2 — return of morning impulsivity, one large meltdown on 18 December triggered by an unexpected change in Christmas shopping plans — with adaptation by days 3–4 as the family held routine firm. The family wedding (20 December) proceeded better than anticipated; Ellie wore her noise-cancelling headphones during the reception and elected to sit with her grandmother rather than the 'loud kids' table, which mother reports as evidence of growing self-awareness. Appetite recovered strongly during the holiday — mother estimates intake doubled on medication-free days. Weight recorded at home on 21 December: 20.0 kg. Medication resumed 22 December as planned.

Since resumption, Ellie has had approximately four weeks on the 7.5 mg split-dose regimen. Mother's principal new concern is a re-emergent prolongation of sleep onset. Over the past fortnight, mother reports bedtime-to-sleep latency has returned to the 60–90 minute range on most school nights, with Ellie asking to 'read a bit more' and sometimes appearing physically unable to settle.

Differential for the sleep-onset lengthening:
1. Timing of the midday dose: 12:00 dose duration-of-effect extends to approximately 16:00, but individual variation can push this later. If the midday dose effect persists into the 17:00–19:00 window, activation at bedtime is plausible.
2. Year 2 transition anxiety: Mother has noted Ellie asking about the new classroom, new teacher, and whether the 'quiet corner' will exist in Year 2. Psychology (Amit S.) has addressed this in session and is continuing.
3. Non-pharmacological factors: Increased screen time over the holiday break (acknowledged by mother), delayed sunset, later evening activity.

Examination: Alert and engaged. Weight 20.3 kg (centile stable at 50th; recovered from post-holiday baseline and then some). Height 117.1 cm. BP 100/68. HR 90 regular. No tic movements. No cardiac findings.

Impression: Successful medication holiday with expected rebound and recovery. Current regimen tolerated. Sleep-onset latency prolongation likely multifactorial — pharmacological contribution plausible but not the sole driver.

Plan:
1. Continue immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg mane + 2.5 mg at 12:00. Total daily dose 7.5 mg.
2. Explicitly eliminate any doses administered after 13:00. Mother and school nurse to confirm midday dose is given no later than 12:30. If the 12:00 dose is missed at school, the midday dose is to be omitted rather than given late.
3. Sleep hygiene: Re-establish wind-down routine after a holiday-relaxed regime. Screen cut-off at 19:00 on school nights. Low-stimulation lighting from 19:00. Short quiet-activity window before bed (reading, low-intensity audio).
4. Monitor sleep onset across next fortnight. If onset latency remains >60 minutes consistently, consider trial of melatonin 3 mg at 19:30 (to be prescribed only if non-pharmacological measures fail).
5. Continue OT (Jess P.) and psychology (Amit S.).
6. Year 2 transition: Mother reassured — engage Amit on anticipated anxiety, encourage a classroom visit pre-start if school permits.
7. Review in 8 weeks (2026-03-17).

Safety-netting reiterated. Copy to: Dr L. Tran, Dr R. Meyer, Jess P., Amit S., family file.

author: Dr L. Tran (GP)
title: After-hours consult — abdominal pain
text:
AFTER-HOURS GP CONSULT — Ellie, 6y 8m.

Date/time: 2026-02-19, 08:45. Attending: Mother. Ellie presented alert and walking but subdued.

Presenting complaint: Acute onset of mid-abdominal pain in the early hours of 2026-02-19 (approximately 02:00), waking Ellie from sleep. Mother reports Ellie cried intermittently for approximately 60 minutes, localising the pain to the periumbilical area. No associated fever, no vomiting, no diarrhoea, no haematuria, no bleeding per rectum. Last bowel motion: four days prior to presentation, described by mother as 'small, hard'. Pain resolved spontaneously by 03:30; Ellie slept until morning. This is the first episode of this kind.

Background: ADHD-C on immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg mane + 2.5 mg at 12:00 (7.5 mg/day). Ongoing appetite suppression on medication. Known sensory-selective eating. Otherwise well.

On examination: Afebrile, T 36.7°C. HR 82. RR 18. BP 102/66. Alert, interactive, able to climb onto the examination couch unassisted.
Abdomen: Soft, not distended. Mild tenderness periumbilically on deep palpation without guarding or rebound. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds active, normal character. No flank tenderness. Hernial orifices intact. Rectal examination not indicated at this stage.
Chest: Clear.
ENT: No signs of concurrent viral illness.
Hydration: Clinically euvolaemic.

Differential diagnosis:
1. Constipation — most likely. Stimulant-associated reduced appetite and fluid intake, combined with selective eating and a reported 4-day interval since last motion, all fit. Clinical picture is not acute.
2. Mesenteric adenitis — possible but no recent viral prodrome described.
3. Acute appendicitis — no localisation to right iliac fossa, no guarding, no fever, child non-toxic. Low suspicion but worth communicating safety-netting.
4. Urinary tract infection — no dysuria, no frequency, no fever. Dipstick performed in clinic, negative.
5. Functional abdominal pain — possible, would be a diagnosis of exclusion.

Management:
- Reassurance to mother.
- Dietary advice: Increase fluid intake explicitly (target 1.2–1.4 L/day for a child of this size). Add a structured fibre source — pear, kiwi fruit, a bran breakfast cereal. I have written the fibre-plan on a handout.
- Osmotic laxative: Prescribed Movicol Paediatric, half a sachet daily for 7 days, titrate to a soft, easily passed daily motion. This is a low-threshold intervention given the likely mechanism; family agreed.
- No change to stimulant medication at this stage. Dr Kostas to be notified; any adjustment is his decision.
- Mother to continue to log bowel motions and any recurrent pain episodes.

Safety-netting (written and verbal):
- Return immediately or attend ED if pain recurs with any of: high fever, vomiting, localisation to right lower abdomen, refusal to walk, lethargy, blood in stool, or abdominal distension.
- Review in one week to confirm resolution of constipation pattern.

Follow-up: Review booked 2026-02-26. Letter to Dr Kostas under separate cover flagging constipation concern in the context of stimulant therapy.

Dr L. Tran, MBBS, FRACGP.

author: Dr A. Kostas (Paediatrician)
title: Consultant letter to school — reasonable adjustments
text:
CONSULTANT LETTER — Reasonable Adjustments.

To: Principal and Year 2 Classroom Team, Brunswick Primary School.
Re: Ellie (Year 2), Student ID [redacted].
From: Dr A. Kostas, Consultant Paediatrician.

To the Principal and Year 2 teaching team,

I am writing at the request of Ellie's mother, and with her written consent, to outline clinically indicated reasonable adjustments for Ellie for the 2026 school year. This letter supersedes any prior informal arrangements and may be kept on file for reference by any relieving or specialist teacher who works with Ellie.

Clinical summary (shared on a need-to-know basis): Ellie has a confirmed diagnosis of Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder, combined presentation (ADHD-C), formally confirmed by a consultant child psychiatrist in November 2025. She has co-occurring sensory processing differences (primarily auditory and tactile). She is under my ongoing paediatric care, concurrent occupational therapy (Jess P., Northcote Therapy Collective), and psychology (Amit S., Kensington Psychology Clinic). She is currently treated with immediate-release methylphenidate; this is discussed in a separate section below.

Recommended reasonable adjustments:

1. Classroom seating and environment
- Seating away from high-traffic areas (doors, windows onto the playground, loudly ticking clocks, the classroom sink). A position toward the front of the room but not in the main line of sight of external distractions is typically optimal.
- Predictable, visually uncluttered workstation. A short personal checklist visible at her desk supports task initiation and transitions.

2. Sensory regulation
- Permitted access to noise-reducing headphones during formal assessments, school assemblies, and any classroom activity with sustained noise levels (music lessons, performances, indoor PE). These do not impair instruction — Ellie reliably removes them when a teacher addresses the class.
- A 'sensory break' card that Ellie can present to the teacher to request a 3–5 minute break in a pre-agreed quiet space (the reading corner, the library, a designated bench). She has been taught to use this card in session with her occupational therapist and is capable of exercising it appropriately.
- Permitted use of a chewable necklace and a small fidget (non-disruptive) during seated work.

3. Movement and transitions
- Scheduled movement opportunities across the day. 'Heavy work' micro-tasks (carrying a stack of books to the office, stacking chairs) are particularly regulating for Ellie and typically welcomed by her.
- Five-minute warning before transitions where possible. Ellie's OT has given the family and teacher a visual timer strategy that she is familiar with.

4. Assessment and performance anxiety
- Emergent perfectionism has been flagged by her psychologist and is being actively addressed. Consider low-stakes framing of assessments, and be alert to sustained distress (tearfulness, refusal, 'I'm stupid' statements) following perceived mistakes. A brief, matter-of-fact teacher response is generally more helpful than reassurance.

5. Medication administration
- Ellie is prescribed immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg at home prior to school, and 2.5 mg at 12:00 to be administered at school through the school nurse under the school's standing medication register. This is the maximum school-administered dose.
- No further doses are required during school hours.
- If the 12:00 dose is missed for any reason, it should be omitted, not given late — do not administer the midday dose after 13:00 under any circumstances as this has demonstrably affected her sleep when delayed.
- Brief post-dose observation for 20 minutes by the nurse is adequate; no specific monitoring parameters required unless Ellie reports new symptoms.

6. Communication
- I welcome school communication on any emergent concern. Please liaise through the family in the first instance. My rooms are available for direct contact where that is clinically appropriate and the family consents.

I am grateful for the school's demonstrated willingness to engage with reasonable adjustments. Ellie is a bright, curious child who does well when the environment is well-matched to her profile; the adjustments above are the means, not the end.

Yours sincerely,
Dr A. Kostas, FRACP (Paediatrics)
Copy to: Dr L. Tran, family, Ellie's IEP file.

author: Dr R. Meyer (Child Psychiatrist)
title: 6-month psychiatric review
text:
SIX-MONTH PSYCHIATRIC REVIEW — Ellie, 6y 9m.

Attending: Mother. Father joined by telephone for the final 15 minutes of consultation — first psychiatric contact.

Reason for review: Scheduled six-month review following initial psychiatric assessment of 2025-11-02. Clarification of treatment response, monitoring for emergent psychopathology, and consideration of any pharmacological adjustments.

Interval history (past 5 months):

ADHD symptoms: Mother reports substantive functional improvement. Homework completion has become routine rather than confrontational. Morning routine is sustained on most school days with the OT-designed checklist (with some drift, which the OT is iterating on). Classroom engagement has improved per repeated teacher feedback — Ms Harper (Year 2 coordinator, previously Year 1 teacher) emails monthly and has consistently described a child who is 'asking for what she needs' and 'participating in class discussions'. The quantitative signal: Ms Harper reports a doubling of observed hand-raising and voluntary contributions between Term 4 2025 and Term 1 2026.

Sensory and OT progress: Jess P. (OT) has been consistently engaged. Morning checklist in place. Classroom self-advocacy established — Ellie uses her sensory-break card autonomously, reportedly twice daily per teacher observation. Chewy necklace embedded. Handwriting tolerance extended (12 minutes sustained, per Jess's Term 1 review note).

Psychology: Ellie has been seen by Amit S. (Psychologist) since January 2026. Principal work has been around emotion-labelling, transition anxiety (Year 2 start), and — most recently — perfectionism. Ellie now voluntarily uses words like 'frustrated' at home (mother's first documentation in a log entry in early December 2025; has become more consistent since). Sleepover anxiety has been worked on via a graded-exposure ladder and is progressing.

Emergent perfectionism: This is the principal new theme since the last review. Mother describes school-based distress following minor errors — most memorably a 15-minute tearful episode following an 8/10 on a Year 2 spelling test, with Ellie perseverating on the two words she had missed rather than on the eight she had got right. Pattern is consistent across home (homework mistakes) and school (assessment, social-error repair). Psychologist has initiated perfectionism-specific work and is in dialogue with the family around scripts and parental modelling.

Sleep: Earlier concern about sleep-onset latency (up to 90 minutes, documented in DL-006, 2026-01-20) has resolved. Mother explicitly attributes this to the elimination of any methylphenidate doses after 13:00. Onset latency now 20–30 minutes, within age-appropriate range.

Appetite and growth: Stable. Weight 20.5 kg (plotted, 50th centile line maintained). Dietitian referral not required.

Medication: Continues on immediate-release methylphenidate 5 mg mane + 2.5 mg at 12:00. Tolerability good.

Somatic: One episode of acute abdominal pain (DL-007, 2026-02-19) — attributed to stimulant-associated constipation by Dr Tran, resolved on osmotic laxative and dietary adjustment, no recurrence of severity. A milder episode was noted on 2026-03-18 per family log, self-limiting, no medical contact required.

Mental state examination (today): Appearance appropriate, relaxed. Psychomotor activity within normal limits for age — notably less restless than at the November 2025 assessment. Speech unremarkable. Mood 'good'. Affect euthymic, reactive. Thought content — no obsessional content, no delusional content, no self-harm or suicidal ideation. Perception unremarkable. Cognition — attentive in session throughout a 45-minute interview, a notable change. Insight and judgement developmentally appropriate.

Formulation: Ellie continues to meet criteria for ADHD-C on clinical history, with good response to combined pharmacological, occupational-therapy, and psychological intervention. No features of a co-occurring anxiety disorder meeting DSM-5 threshold at this time — perfectionism is the principal area requiring clinical attention but does not yet represent OCD, generalised anxiety disorder, or a specific phobia on structured history. No features of autism spectrum disorder. No features of a mood disorder.

Plan:
1. No pharmacological change. Continuation of current regimen indicated.
2. Continue psychology (Amit S.) at current fortnightly cadence. Perfectionism work to continue as a priority.
3. Consider a single parent-directed psychology session with Amit to coach perfectionism scripts — mother receptive, will self-book.
4. Continue OT (Jess P.) at current fortnightly cadence.
5. Continue paediatric review with Dr Kostas as scheduled.
6. Next psychiatric review in 6 months (2026-10-01) or sooner at family or referrer request. Mother has my rooms' contact details and the after-hours crisis line.
7. I would not recommend SSRI or other pharmacotherapy for perfectionism at this stage. Recommend re-evaluation in 6 months; lower threshold for pharmacological discussion if perfectionism becomes functionally impairing (school refusal, daily tearfulness, social withdrawal).

Copy to: Dr L. Tran, Dr A. Kostas, Jess P., Amit S., family file.

author: Jess P. (OT)
title: Initial sensory profile
text: Completed Sensory Profile 2 (caregiver questionnaire) and clinic observation. Ellie scores in the 'much more than others' range for Sensory Sensitivity and Sensory Avoiding. Auditory and tactile channels are primary. Strong seeking behaviour for proprioceptive input (jumping, crashing, deep pressure). Plan: weekly sessions for 6 weeks. Goals: (1) classroom self-advocacy for sensory breaks, (2) morning routine checklist, (3) mother coached on proprioceptive 'heavy work' at home.

author: Jess P. (OT)
title: Session 2 — handwriting grip
text: Observed tripod grip with excessive pressure, fatigue after 5 minutes. Trialled weighted pencil and rubber grip — Ellie preferred the soft grip. Introduced pre-writing hand warm-up (theraputty). Took home a grip and a putty pot. Mother to do 2 min warm-up before homework.

author: Jess P. (OT)
title: Session 3 — iPad transition strategy
text: Worked on transitions from preferred screen activity to non-preferred tasks. Co-designed a visual countdown timer system with Ellie. She chose a dinosaur theme. Mother trialling at home. Next week: review what worked, iterate.

author: Amit S. (Psychologist)
title: Session 1 — emotion vocabulary
text: First session. Rapport-building via Lego. Introduced a feelings chart with six primary emotions and short descriptors. Ellie identified 'frustrated' and 'excited' readily; 'disappointed' and 'overwhelmed' took scaffolding. Homework: mother to label two feelings per day using the chart.

author: Jess P. (OT)
title: Session 4 — chewy trial
text: Parent query — collar chewing at school. Trialled two chewy necklaces in clinic. Ellie preferred the softer grey one. Ordered at home. Discussed school permission — liaison letter to be sent.

author: Amit S. (Psychologist)
title: Session 2 — emotion labelling progress
text: Mother reports Ellie spontaneously used 'frustrated' at home re sibling conflict — significant. In session, Ellie mapped a recent event (school concert) to three feelings in sequence (excited → overwhelmed → sad). Introduced basic regulation scripts. Progress positive.

author: Jess P. (OT)
title: Session 5 — classroom self-advocacy
text: Role-played the 'sensory break card' system with Ellie. She practised raising the card silently to Ms Harper. Liaison with Mr Doyle to embed in classroom. Goal for term 1 Year 2: autonomous break requests.

author: Dana K. (Speech Pathologist)
title: Pragmatic language — initial
text: Assessment session. Narrative and semantic language broadly age-appropriate. Pragmatic domain — difficulty maintaining topic in a 3-person conversation, interrupts peers, struggles with non-literal language ('pull your socks up'). Plan: 6 sessions focused on conversational repair and group dynamics. Coordinate with Amit re emotion overlap.

author: Amit S. (Psychologist)
title: Session 3 — sleepover anxiety
text: Mother reports Ellie requesting a sleepover at a peer's house. Unpacked in session. Anxiety primarily about unfamiliar sensory environment (lights, noises). Built a step-ladder: (1) afternoon playdate, (2) dinner and stay until 8pm, (3) full sleepover. Mother to coordinate with peer's parent.

author: Jess P. (OT)
title: Session 6 — Year 2 transition review
text: Mother reports classroom-visit strategy (pre-term) worked well. Ellie chose her own seat near a window. Discussed the first-week 'mask-crash' pattern at home and strategies for decompression. Recommend a post-school 'quiet window' of 30–45 minutes with no demands.

author: Amit S. (Psychologist)
title: Session 4 — after-school mask-crash
text: Explored the Friday afternoon meltdown pattern. Ellie identified that she 'holds it in' at school and it 'falls out' at home. Normalised and validated. Worked on giving the pattern a name ('the overflow') and planning a predictable after-school routine.

author: Dana K. (Speech Pathologist)
title: Pragmatic language — session 2
text: Worked on conversational repair: noticing when a peer has changed topic, asking rather than interrupting. Used puppet role-play. Ellie engaged well; generalisation to playground remains the work.

author: Amit S. (Psychologist)
title: Session 5 — perfectionism
text: Ellie presented distressed re a spelling test (8/10). Cognitive distortion identified ('I got it wrong = I am bad at spelling'). Introduced the 'OK-different-than-I-wanted' frame. Homework: mother to model the frame when she makes small errors.

author: Jess P. (OT)
title: Term 1 review
text: Ellie using the sensory corner at school autonomously 2x/day per teacher report. Chewy necklace embedded. Morning checklist being used inconsistently (mother reports drop-off after week 3 — common). Reset and iterated. Handwriting grip now sustained for 12 minutes.

author: Amit S. (Psychologist)
title: Session 6 — friendship dynamics
text: Three-way friendship distress (Ellie / Amelia / Zara). Worked on the script: 'I want to play too — can the three of us do X?'. Role-played with stuffed animals. Some resistance, came around. Homework: mother to coach script if it arises.

author: Jess P. (OT)
title: Holiday session — regulation review
text: Holiday session by request. Discussed which sensory strategies generalise to the unstructured holiday environment. Swimming identified as a high-yield activity (proprioceptive + structured). Recommend maintaining weekly swim block into term 2.

author: Amit S. (Psychologist)
title: Session 7 — self-identity
text: Ellie spontaneously discussed being 'wired different'. Affirmed. Explored what she likes about her 'fast brain' (ideas, energy) and what is hard (too much, too loud). Strength-based conversation. Mother reports Ellie initiated this at home too — positive signal.

author: Ms Harper (Year 1 teacher)
title: Quick update — good focus today
text: Hi Maya, just a quick note to say Ellie had a really focused literacy session today. She finished the task and read her story to the group. Wanted you to know. — Jo

author: Ms Harper (Year 1 teacher)
title: Pickup this afternoon
text: Maya, there was an incident at lunch between Ellie and Amelia. Nobody hurt, just some upset feelings around a game. Ellie was quiet in the afternoon and didn't want to talk about it. Flagging so you have context at pickup. — Jo

author: Mr Doyle (Learning support)
title: IEP meeting date
text: Hi Maya, proposing Tuesday 25th at 2:30pm for the IEP review. Ms Harper and I will attend. Let me know if that works. — Tom

author: Ms Harper (Year 1 teacher)
title: Good week
text: Just wanted to send a positive note — Ellie had a great week. Used her break card once on Thursday (very proud of her), finished all her maths, made a lovely card for Amelia's birthday. Jo

author: Mrs Ng (SENCO)
title: Sensory break card — embedded
text: Hi Maya, the break card is now part of Ellie's classroom routine across all teachers. She's used it 3–4 times this fortnight. Mr Doyle will continue to monitor. Let me know if anything from home suggests we need to adjust. — Priya

author: Ms Harper (Year 1 teacher)
title: Concert logistics
text: Quick one — we'll seat Ellie near the aisle so she can step out if the concert gets overwhelming. Mr Doyle will be nearby. Wanted you to know the plan. — Jo

author: Mr Doyle (Learning support)
title: Year 2 first week wrap
text: Hi Maya, solid first week. Ellie chose her seat, introduced herself to the new teacher calmly, and used the sensory corner once on Wednesday. She seems to like the more structured Year 2 routine. — Tom

author: Mrs Ng (SENCO)
title: Reasonable adjustments — paed letter received
text: Hi Maya, received Dr Kostas's letter. Adjustments are already in place. I've circulated to Ellie's Year 2 teacher and specialist teachers (PE, music, art). No action needed from you. — Priya

author: Ms Harper (Year 1 teacher, now Year 2 coordinator)
title: Friendship update
text: Hi Maya, noticed Ellie has had some friction with Zara this week around a three-way dynamic with Amelia. Nothing escalated, but she was quiet at Thursday lunch. Flagging so you can check in at home. — Jo

author: Mr Doyle (Learning support)
title: Excursion prep — zoo
text: Hi Maya, zoo excursion is Monday. Bus leaves 9am, back 3pm. Ellie will have her headphones and chewy. I'll be her point of contact. Long day — she may be flat afterwards. — Tom

author: Ms Harper
title: Spelling test feedback
text: Maya, Ellie had a hard time with the spelling test result on Friday (8/10 — strong score). She was upset about the two she missed. I gave her space in the sensory corner and she rejoined the group. Might be worth mentioning to Amit. — Jo

author: Mrs Ng (SENCO)
title: Term 1 wrap — IEP summary
text: Hi Maya, end-of-term summary: Ellie has met 3/4 IEP goals (sensory self-advocacy, morning routine, task completion). Goal 4 (peer conflict repair) is partially met — progressing. IEP review next term will focus there. Brief attached. — Priya

author: Mr Doyle (Learning support)
title: Welcome back — term 2
text: Hi Maya, ready for Ellie to start term 2 on Monday. Same seat, same plan. Let me know if anything shifted over the holidays that I should know about before then. — Tom
